items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’ve got that nagging feeling again...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
and the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core comedic effect is mock-heroic/highbrow phrasing applied to petty suspicion, plus the elliptical parallel structure. The translation partially preserves the suspicion idea but weakens the elevated register: “nagging feeling” is too everyday and loses the theatrical grandiosity of «терзают смутные сомнения». More importantly, it misrepresents the meaning of «у Шпака — …, у посла — …»: it’s not possession (“has”) but association/attribution in context of missing items (each has had that item involved/lost). This shifts the logic of the joke and the callback. A better rendering would keep the lofty tone (“vague doubts haunt me…”) and avoid “has” (e.g., “Shpak—tape recorder; the ambassador—medallion.”).

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Tsar—pleased to meet you, hello.
Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The humor is the repetitive, banal Soviet-style politeness and the absurd insistence on “Tsar” as if it were a job title/name tag. The translation preserves repetition and awkward social-cliché rhythm well. It slightly underplays the self-introduction nuance (the reference’s “I’m the Tsar” makes the social faux pas clearer), but the effect still lands because “Hello, Tsar” repeated is similarly ridiculous. Overall acceptable and funny.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference; preserves the sudden authoritarian, pompous demand and the comic escalation perfectly.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me be, old woman—I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Captures the elevated, archaic melancholy register and the dismissive address “old woman.” The slight modernization (“Leave me be” + dash) is fine for subtitles and keeps the tone. No meaningful loss of comedic contrast.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is broadly conveyed (he believes he’s been sealed in by demons), but the English is clipped in a way that distorts the syntax and weakens the gag. “Bricked up” reads like a modern slangy adjective/phrase and can misfire; it also loses the passive victimhood (“They’ve walled me in”) that makes the accusation funnier. Missing the ellipsis/subject also reduces the melodramatic lament quality.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and idiomatic. It keeps the religious framing and the sense of marvel. Slightly less mock-biblical/ceremonial than “Behold the power…”, but still works and remains clearly ‘Tsar-ish’ in tone.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Conveys the basic directive but loses the specific comic flavor: in Russian it’s a showman/MC-style formula (“Everyone’s dancing!” / “All dance!”) that feels like an announcement, not a direct command. “Everybody dance!” is a blunt imperative and less ‘variety-show’/ceremonial. Still understandable and functional, but the performative, era-clash vibe is reduced.

items.translation: What a perfect time to drop in!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the opportunistic satisfaction and the breezy criminal cheer. “Perfect time to drop in” is a reasonable colloquial equivalent; it keeps the self-congratulatory tone. Slightly less punchy than the reference, but the comedic beat remains.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference; preserves the key creative decision not to call it “caviar” in English and keeps the punchline structure.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Three tape recorders, three movie cameras,
three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The list-building and escalating-count humor is mostly preserved. However, the reference’s hesitations/ellipses (“domestic…”, “a suede jacket… Three.”) are part of the comic performance: Shpak is improvising and inflating. The actual translation smooths it slightly (“three domestic cigarette cases”) and removes some of the stumbly rhythm, reducing the sense that he’s making it up as he goes. Still clear and funny enough.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you...
and they’ll cure you too...
and they’ll cure me...
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Essentially identical in effect; preserves the calm, rhythmic resignation and the shared-absurdity punch. Line breaks are subtitle-formatting only.

summary.interpretation: 1.91 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.38 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
